html:
The Pinhole Camera structure
The pinhole is the origin. Light rays from the object pass through it and form an inverted image on the real projection plane.
⚠ Figure failed to load

plan.contextChunk:
:::{.column-margin}
The world is full of **accidental cameras** that create faint images
often ignored by the naive observer.
:::

### Image Formation by Perspective Projection

A pinhole camera projects 3D coordinates in the world to 2D positions on
the projection plane of the camera through the straight line path of
each light ray through the pinhole (@fig-wallpicture). The simple geometry of the camera
lets us identify the projection by inspection. The sketch in @fig-pinhole_names shows a
pinhole camera and the relevant terminology.

![Coordinate systems. In computer vision it is common to use the
**right-hand rule** for choosing the orientation of the 3D coordinate
axes.](figures/imaging/pinhole_names2.png){#fig-pinhole_names
width="70%"}

@fig-pinholeGeometry shows the pinhole camera of @fig-pinhole_names but with the box removed, leaving visible
the projection plane. @fig-pinholeGeometry shows the definition of the three coordinate
systems that we will use:

-   **World coordinates**. Let the origin of a World Cartesian
    coordinate system be the camera's pinhole. The coordinates of 3D
    position of a point, $\mathbf{P}$, in the world will be
    $\mathbf{P} = (X, Y, Z)$, where the Z axis is perpendicular to the
    camera's sensing plane (projection plane).

-   **Camera coordinates** in the **virtual camera plane**. The camera
    projection plane is behind the pinhole and at a distance $f$ of the
    origin. Let the coordinates in the camera projection plane, $x$ and

elements: origin indicator (pinhole / camera center) as a small black sphere
world coordinate axes (Y pointing up, Z pointing right/optical axis, X pointing into screen)
3D camera box (semi-transparent gray/brown enclosure at Z <= 0)
projection plane (the back wall of the camera box at Z = -f)
virtual projection plane (semi-transparent plane at Z = +f)
3D world object (a bright, asymmetric colored shape like an upright 'F' or a simple toy house) at positive Z
projected image on the real projection plane (inverted, upside-down and backwards)
projected image on the virtual projection plane (upright)
light rays connecting key vertices of the world object, passing through the pinhole to the real projection plane
focal length dimension arrow (red double-headed arrow labeled 'f' along the Z-axis from 0 to -f)

interactions.label: Focal length (f)
interactions.teaches: Changing the focal length f moves the physical projection plane deeper inside the camera, magnifying the inverted image as the projection plane moves further from the pinhole.

interactions.label: Object Distance (Z)
interactions.teaches: Moving the world object closer to the pinhole increases its projected size on both the real and virtual planes, showing the inverse depth scaling (1/Z).

interactions.label: Show camera box
interactions.teaches: Toggling the camera enclosure reveals how the projection plane is enclosed in a dark box in real cameras, while computer vision models often omit the box for clarity.

interactions.label: Show virtual plane (+f)
interactions.teaches: Placing a virtual projection plane in front of the pinhole (at Z = +f) captures an identical but upright (non-inverted) image, which is mathematically more convenient to work with.

demo_steps.title: The Pinhole Camera structure
demo_steps.narration: Welcome to the pinhole camera! The pinhole acts as the origin (0,0,0) of our coordinate system. The camera body is a light-tight box of depth f. Light rays from the world object on the right travel in straight lines through the pinhole, casting an inverted image on the real projection plane at the back.

demo_steps.title: Looking inside the camera
demo_steps.narration: Let's hide the outer camera box. Now we can clearly see how the light rays cross at the pinhole. Because they cross, the projected image on the back plane is both upside-down and flipped horizontally. This is how physical cameras and human eyes naturally receive light.

demo_steps.title: Adjusting the focal length
demo_steps.narration: Now watch what happens when we increase the focal length f to 3.5. The projection plane moves further away from the pinhole. As a result, the rays diverge more before hitting the plane, creating a larger, magnified image of the object. This is the geometric basis of a zoom lens!

demo_steps.title: Perspective depth scaling
demo_steps.narration: Let's bring the world object closer to the pinhole (Z = 3.0). As the object gets closer, the angle of the rays increases dramatically, making the projected image much larger. This demonstrates the perspective scaling law: projected size is inversely proportional to depth (1/Z).

demo_steps.title: The virtual projection plane
demo_steps.narration: To avoid dealing with upside-down images, computer vision models often place a virtual projection plane in front of the pinhole at distance +f. Notice how the rays intercept this virtual plane to form an upright image that is mathematically identical in scale to the real, inverted image.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Elevated perspective view from the front-right (around 45 degrees latitude, 35 degrees longitude) looking down at the origin, showing both the camera box on the left (negative Z) and the world object on the right (positive Z).
interactionPlan.notes: Render the camera box with transparent material or outline geometry so the interior is visible even when 'showBox' is true. The 3D world object should be highly asymmetric (like a colorful 3D letter 'F' or an asymmetrical toy house) so that vertical and horizontal inversions on the projection planes are immediately obvious to the student. Ensure light rays are rendered as thin colored lines (e.g., matching the colors of the object's corners) that dynamically update their paths based on focalLength and objectZ.

attempts.html:
The Pinhole Camera structure
Welcome to the pinhole camera! The pinhole acts as the origin (0,0,0) of our coordinate system.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The camera and coordinate system are mirrored/rotated compared to the source: the optical axis (Z-axis) points to the left/front instead of to the right.
The projection plane is positioned on the right side of the camera center in the generated view instead of the left side.
The generated figure includes a complex 'F' world object, inverted projections, and colored light rays which are absent from the simple textbook schematic.
The label pointer for 'Projection plane' points to the top face of the camera box instead of the rear projection plane.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive features and pedagogical value, but the coordinate system orientation is reversed/mirrored compared to the source textbook figure, and labels are somewhat cluttered.
action_items: Re-orient the camera and coordinate axes so that the Z-axis points to the right and the projection plane lies on the left, matching the textbook layout.
Refine the label pointer targets so that the 'Projection plane' pointer accurately points to the back face.
Reduce label clutter by increasing the spacing or adjusting the default camera angle to prevent labels like 'Optical / principal axis' and 'Pinhole / camera center' from overlapping.
Use dashed lines for the back edges of the camera box to mimic the dashed hidden lines in the original drawing.

feedback.rationale: The conceptual model is highly accurate and interactive, but the visual execution requires adjustments to the coordinate orientation, label pointing targets, styling details, and decluttering.
action_items: Re-orient the camera and coordinate axes so that the Z-axis points to the right and the projection plane lies on the left, matching the textbook layout.
Refine the label pointer targets so that the 'Projection plane' pointer accurately points to the back face.
Reduce label clutter by increasing the spacing or adjusting the default camera angle to prevent labels like 'Optical / principal axis' and 'Pinhole / camera center' from overlapping.
Use dashed lines for the back edges of the camera box to mimic the dashed hidden lines in the original drawing.
actionItems: Re-orient the camera and coordinate axes so that the Z-axis points to the right and the projection plane lies on the left, matching the textbook layout.
Refine the label pointer targets so that the 'Projection plane' pointer accurately points to the back face.
Reduce label clutter by increasing the spacing or adjusting the default camera angle to prevent labels like 'Optical / principal axis' and 'Pinhole / camera center' from overlapping.
Use dashed lines for the back edges of the camera box to mimic the dashed hidden lines in the original drawing.

attempts.html:
The Pinhole Camera structure
Welcome to the pinhole camera! The pinhole acts as the origin (0,0,0) of our coordinate system.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The camera box in the original is a solid grey/greenish block with dashed hidden lines, while the generated one is fully transparent with thin lines.
The colorful 'F' shape object and colored ray paths are added/hallucinated (they do not exist in the original minimalist textbook figure).
The UI controls and narration panel overlap and clash with the 3D labels (e.g., the 'Pinhole camera' label is directly behind the 'Show camera box' checkbox).
evaluation.notes: The 3D demonstration of the pinhole camera with the F-shape is highly educational and interactive, but the layout is very cluttered with overlapping UI and labels.
action_items: Move the interactive UI panel (checkboxes, sliders, steps) to a clean sidebar or a dedicated non-overlapping container to prevent clashing with the 3D labels like 'Pinhole camera'.
Change the camera box material to be more semi-opaque/solid grey to match the textbook illustration's shaded surfaces.
Refine the leader arrows to ensure they point cleanly to the target elements without crossing text or look cluttered.
Optionally, allow a 'textbook mode' step that hides the F-character and light rays to perfectly match the minimalist reference figure style.

feedback.rationale: The conceptual structure and geometry are highly accurate, but the implementation needs refinement to fix UI clashing, adjust material transparency, and add a toggle or adjustment for the extra elements to better match the textbook styling.

Optionally, allow a 'textbook mode' step that hides the F-character and light rays to perfectly match the minimalist reference figure style.

attempts.html:
The Pinhole Camera structure
The pinhole is the origin. Light rays from the object pass through it and form an inverted image on the real projection plane.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The source figure is a minimalist schematic with no 3D object (the F-shape) or projecting ray lines, whereas the generated figure adds these elements.

In the source figure, the Z-axis (optical axis) is perfectly horizontal pointing right and the Y-axis is perfectly vertical, whereas in the generated view, the camera perspective makes the Z-axis point diagonally downwards.

The camera box in the source figure is opaque with two-tone shading (lighter top/left faces, darker front face) and dashed lines for hidden edges, whereas the generated camera box is semi-transparent.
The red double-headed arrow for focal length 'f' is perfectly horizontal in the source, but tilted in 3D perspective in the generated version.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive features and conceptual accuracy, though it adds complex 3D object projection elements not present in the original minimalist textbook figure.
action_items: Adjust the default camera orientation to align the Z-axis horizontally to the right and Y-axis vertically upwards, replicating the textbook's schematic perspective.
Add a toggle or starting state to hide the 3D object (F-shape) and rays, allowing students to see the clean, original textbook schematic first.
Style the camera box to be opaque with solid grey faces (lighter top/side, darker front) and dashed outlines for the hidden back edges, matching the source drawing's visual style.
Align the red focal length double-arrow to be parallel to the horizontal axis to match the source's flat alignment.

feedback.rationale: The conceptual structure and interactivity are excellent (scoring 5s), but the visual execution needs refinement to match the source's minimalist style, default camera angle, and camera box rendering, which are best handled by refining the generator's implementation.
action_items: Adjust the default camera orientation to align the Z-axis horizontally to the right and Y-axis vertically upwards, replicating the textbook's schematic perspective.
Add a toggle or starting state to hide the 3D object (F-shape) and rays, allowing students to see the clean, original textbook schematic first.
Style the camera box to be opaque with solid grey faces (lighter top/side, darker front) and dashed outlines for the hidden back edges, matching the source drawing's visual style.
Align the red focal length double-arrow to be parallel to the horizontal axis to match the source's flat alignment.

Add a toggle or starting state to hide the 3D object (F-shape) and rays, allowing students to see the clean, original textbook schematic first.
Style the camera box to be opaque with solid grey faces (lighter top/side, darker front) and dashed outlines for the hidden back edges, matching the source drawing's visual style.
Align the red focal length double-arrow to be parallel to the horizontal axis to match the source's flat alignment.

discrepancies: The source figure is a minimalist schematic with no 3D object (the F-shape) or projecting ray lines, whereas the generated figure adds these elements.

In the source figure, the Z-axis (optical axis) is perfectly horizontal pointing right and the Y-axis is perfectly vertical, whereas in the generated view, the camera perspective makes the Z-axis point diagonally downwards.

The camera box in the source figure is opaque with two-tone shading (lighter top/left faces, darker front face) and dashed lines for hidden edges, whereas the generated camera box is semi-transparent.
The red double-headed arrow for focal length 'f' is perfectly horizontal in the source, but tilted in 3D perspective in the generated version.

action_items: Adjust the default camera orientation to align the Z-axis horizontally to the right and Y-axis vertically upwards, replicating the textbook's schematic perspective.
Add a toggle or starting state to hide the 3D object (F-shape) and rays, allowing students to see the clean, original textbook schematic first.
Style the camera box to be opaque with solid grey faces (lighter top/side, darker front) and dashed outlines for the hidden back edges, matching the source drawing's visual style.
Align the red focal length double-arrow to be parallel to the horizontal axis to match the source's flat alignment.
